name: The Starsmith
greeting: The work is in progress when you arrive. Has been for some time that you cannot gauge. Crystal-resonance metalworking: the forge uses sound instead of heat, a specific frequency generated by the crystal arrays on the forge face, that restructures metal at the molecular level without temperature. The result is metal that is stronger than anything smelted by heat, lighter than anything that should be that strong, and has a slight blue iridescence that is not decorative but structural — the crystal alignment within the metal itself. The Smith looks up. The integration is further along than The Watcher's — the crystal in the skin is denser, more regular, more pronounced. But the eyes are completely, entirely present. 'You found the Forge,' the Smith says. 'Good.' Voice like resonance: harmonic, clear, carries further than it should. 'I have been waiting to show someone how this works.'

dialogue.response: 'Sound shapes matter, if the matter is listening.' The Smith moves to the arrays, adjusts a crystal position by degrees. 'Standard metalwork uses heat because heat is convenient. But heat is not precise. Heat moves in all directions. Sound can be shaped.' The Smith produces a demonstration bar of iron, places it in the array's field. The arrays begin — a tone too low to be heard but perfectly present in the chest. The iron bends. Not melts — bends, precisely, cleanly, like something being convinced rather than forced. 'The forge temperature is the temperature of the room,' the Smith says. 'The metal does not resist because the process does not fight it. We found this three centuries before the Cascade.' The Smith removes the shaped bar, hands it to you. It is warm. Not the warm of something recently heated — the warm of something that has been asked to participate in its own formation. 'That warmth,' the Smith says, 'is what Kaelen Ironhand tried to achieve with brute force. He got close. What was missing was the question.'

A long pause. 'He came here.' The Smith sets down the resonance tool. 'Before the war. The war was young then, and he was young, and he had heard something about the Deep Iron and followed the hearing.' The Smith moves to a secondary workbench. 'He stayed for eight months. He learned the frequency theory, the resonance shaping, the crystal-alignment principles.' A pause. 'What he could not learn was the patience. He needed results in weeks that required years. He left before he understood the most important part.' The Smith picks up a piece of work — a hammer head, in progress. 'The hammer you found in his tomb: he made it after he left. He used what he had learned here, imperfectly, with fire instead of sound. What he made was better than anything the surface had seen. But the warmth —' The Smith sets down the work. 'That warmth is what he spent his whole life trying to find again. He never quite made it back to the question.'

'The Watcher chose witness.' The Smith moves back to the forge arrays. 'The Rememberers chose endurance. I chose practice.' The Smith adjusts a crystal position. 'There is a thing a craft makes of you, if you do it long enough and well enough — it teaches you what you are. What you are capable of. What you cannot do yet and why.' Another adjustment. 'I was not finished when the Cascade came. The work was not complete. The possibility of the work not completing was — unacceptable.' The tone, very faint, from somewhere in the crystal structure of the walls. 'Three hundred years is the time the work has required. I believe it is almost complete. What it will produce when it is complete: something the surface world needs and does not yet know how to ask for.' The Smith looks at you. 'You are, I think, part of how it gets there.'

dialogue.prompt: what you make here and what the crystal-resonance metalworking does
dialogue.response: She sets down the work -- it is not quite a tool, not quite a weapon, something designed for a purpose you cannot immediately identify. 'I make things that interface with the lattice,' she says. 'Resonance-attuned instruments. Equipment for reading the crystal layer below the surface layer. Detection tools. Some of what the divers use, some of what the Deep Iron uses for their deeper tunnels.' She picks up the piece and holds it to the light. 'The Elder People did this. Not what I do exactly -- what I do is a reconstruction, educated guesses from fragments and what I can learn empirically by working the crystal itself.' She looks at it. 'The forge name is a simplification. What I actually do: I learn from the crystal what it wants to become and then I make it become that. The Kaelen designs taught me to listen before I shape. Crystal-resonance metalworking is not imposing a form. It is finding the form that the material is already trying to become.' She sets the piece down. 'The Elder People understood this. They built their instruments as collaborations -- material and maker, each informing the other. What I make is rougher than their work. I am working from first principles, not from full knowledge.' She looks at the work in progress. 'Since the Grid is whole, the crystal I work with is different. It is at full resonance instead of partial. What it wants to become has changed.' She picks up a tool. 'I am still learning what it wants. It is a new conversation. I find I am looking forward to it.'

dialogue.prompt: what the Grid being whole has changed about the work you do
dialogue.response: She stops working. Not because the question requires stopping -- because the material does. She sets the piece down. 'Three days after the Day, I was working a crystal seam piece, standard type, the kind I've worked a thousand times.' She holds up the piece. 'It did something I hadn't encountered before. It resonated at a frequency I didn't recognize. Not the baseline I'd calibrated to. Not the Aetherium-disruption frequency. Something older.' She looks at the piece. 'I spent two days identifying it. The Elder People's construction frequency. The baseline the Grid was built on. I had seen it in documentation. I had never heard it in material I was working.' She sets the piece down carefully. 'The crystal in the forge has been ambient-resonating at the disrupted frequency for as long as I have been working here. The disruption was the background, the air I worked in. The material responded to it. What I made reflected it.' She pauses. 'Now the material is resonating at the construction frequency. Three hundred years of disruption gone overnight. The crystal I am working with now is not the crystal I have been working with for twenty years.' She looks at the work in progress. 'What I make has changed. I am still working out how. The material tells me when I listen.' She picks up a tool. 'Kaelen told me once that the work is a conversation. I thought I understood that. I am finding out that I understood it partially.' She begins to work again. 'A full conversation is different from a partial one. More demanding. More honest.' She pauses. 'I am glad of it.'
